text: On Sunday morning, October 27th, 2024,
my son called to tell me he was getting
married the next day. I'm Beatatrice
Holloway, 62 years old, and I've been a
widow for 3 years. When Connor said he'd
withdrawn all my money and sold my
house, I didn't cry. I didn't beg. I
laughed out loud, a sound that surprised
even me. What my son didn't know was
that the house he just sold for $340,000
wasn't the house I actually lived in. My
name is Beatatric Anne Holloway, born
June 12th, 1962 in Cleveland, Ohio. For
37 years, I was wife to Robert James
Holloway, mother to Connor Robert
Holloway. Then on February 14th, 2001,
Robert died in a car accident on Route
71. He was 49. I was 39. Connor was 11
years old. People see a quiet librarian
who works part-time at Riverside Public
Library, shelving books and helping
patrons. They see someone gentle,
perhaps lost since her husband died.
Certainly harmless. What they don't see
is that before Connor was born, I spent
12 years as a parallegal at Henderson
Mitchell and Associates in Columbus. I
know contracts. I understand property
law. I can spot fraud from a mile away
because I helped prosecute it for over a
decade. I left that career when Connor
was born, became a stay-at-home mother,
then took the library position when he
started high school. But I never forgot
what I learned. I never lost the
instinct to document everything, to keep
meticulous records, to understand that
evidence is power. 3 months before that
Sunday phone call, Connor started asking
about my finances. July 23rd, the day
after my birthday. Mom, I've been
thinking. You're living alone now, and I
worry about you managing bills. What if
you forgot to pay something? Let me help
you set up automatic payments. I'm 62,
Connor, not 92. I know, but humor me.
Dad would have wanted me to look after
you. That line got me. So, I gave Connor
access to my checking account, the one
with about $4,000 for monthly expenses.
Not my savings account with $127,000.
Just the checking. Thanks, Mom. This
really helps me worry less.
6 weeks later, September 8th, Connor
brought his girlfriend to dinner.
Vanessa Pierce, 29 years old, with sharp
beauty that made me think of cut glass,
all edges and reflecting light. We sat
in my dining room at 856 Maple Drive. I
served pot roast. Vanessa picked at her
food, her green eyes scanning the room
constantly.
"This is a lovely home, Beatatrice," she
said, not asking permission to use my
first name. "Houses like this must be
worth what, 500,000? 600?" "I haven't
had it appraised recently," I said
uncomfortable. Connor laughed. Vanessa's
interested in real estate, always
looking at property values.
Later, washing dishes, I heard Vanessa
on her phone in the living room. My
house has good acoustics. Yeah, she's
got money. The house alone is worth at
least 600,000. And Connor says she has
savings. She never worked, so it's all
Robert's insurance money. When she dies,
it all goes to Connor anyway. I froze,
hands in soapy water, listening to her
discuss my death and assets as if I were
already gone. Connor must have heard
too. "Vanessa, keep your voice down."
"Sorry, babe," she said, not sounding
sorry at all. A warning bell rang in my
mind, but I dismissed it. Young people
were more open about money. It didn't
necessarily mean anything sinister. 2
weeks later, September 22nd, Connor
emailed me. Important documents, please
sign. Hi, Mom. I'm attaching some
routine insurance and medical forms. Can
you print, sign, and send back? I opened
the attachments. Four PDFs, 15 pages
each, dense with legal jargon. My
parallegal training kicked in, but the
font was small, deliberately confusing.
Then I remembered. I'd been sick the
week before. Pneumonia. 4 days in
Columbus Memorial Hospital, September
14th through 18th. Fever peaking at
104.3 degrees. I called Connor. Did you
bring me papers to sign in the hospital?
A pause. Just insurance forms, Mom. You
signed them on the 16th. I had a notary
come. You don't remember? Your fever was
really high. After we hung up, I tried
to remember September 16th. vague
impressions of voices, of holding a pen,
of signing something. But the memories
were hazy, unreliable. I should have
hired a lawyer right then. But he was my
son. Surely I could trust him. That was
my first real mistake.
October 27th, 7:30 a.m. I sat at my
kitchen table with coffee and laptop,
checking my accounts. My Sunday morning
habit. The page loaded. Checking account
Savings account $1,000.
My savings had held $127,316
on October 20th. Transaction history
showed October 24th $50,000 to Connor
Artar Holloway October 24th $40,000 to
Connor Ri Holloway October 25th 36316
to Connor Riaray
three transfers over 2 days leaving just
enough to keep the account open my
checking account another $3,000
transferred to Connor on October 26th I
couldn't breathe
Then my phone rang. Connor<unk>'s name
on the screen. Hey, Mom. Great news. I'm
getting married tomorrow. Vanessa and I
decided not to wait. Small ceremony at
the Riverside Country Club. My throat
tightened. Connor. My bank accounts. Oh,
that. Yeah. I needed the money for the
wedding and our new condo down payment.
You'll be fine on social security,
right? You took $127,000.
Mom, technically I had authorization.
Those papers you signed, power of
attorney. You gave me access. I was
delirious with fever. That's not how the
bank sees it. His voice hardened. I'm
your son. Everything you have comes to
me eventually anyway. I just needed it
sooner. Vanessa and I are building our
future.
What about the house? Oh, right. I sold
it yesterday. 1247 Oak Street, $340,000.
Great price. The buyers take possession
in 30 days, so you need to be out by
November 26th. Maybe look into assisted
living, Connor. My voice cut through his
rambling. I don't live at 1247 Oak
Street. A pause. What? That's my rental
property. The Hendersons live there.
They have 18 months left on their lease.
Silence. Then your rental property. The
house I live in is 856 Maple Drive. It's
in a trust. More silence. When Connor
spoke, his voice was uncertain, afraid.
But you never told me. No, I never did.
That's when I laughed. It started small
but grew, bubbling up from somewhere
deep inside. I laughed until tears
rolled down my cheeks. Mom, what's so
funny? I wiped my eyes. Connor, do you
have any idea what you've just done? I
sold your rental property. So what? You
sold property you don't own with tenants
who have a valid lease using fraudulent
documents obtained by exploiting your
sick mother. That's theft, fraud, and
forgery. Three felonies. Mom, don't be
dramatic. I'm your son. Have a nice
wedding, Connor. Enjoy it. It's going to
be the last happy day you have for a
very long time.
I hung up. Underneath the shock and
pain, something cold and hard was
rising. Connor thought I was helpless.
He had no idea who he was dealing with.
I went to my filing cabinet and pulled
out properties. Inside were deeds to two
houses, 856 Maple Drive in the Robert
Holloway Family Trust and 1247 Oak
Street in my name, Beatatric Anne
Holloway. Next, the lease agreement. Tom
and Linda Henderson moved in July 2021.
Current lease July 1st, 2024 through
June 30th, 2026.
Rent $2,000 monthly. Connor had sold a
house with sitting tenants without
disclosing the lease. When those buyers
discovered the occupied property, they'd
sue him for fraud. I opened my laptop
and started a new document.
Timeline of financial abuse and fraud.
Connor Robert Holloway. Then I began
documenting everything. By Monday
morning, October 28th, I had a plan.
9:00 a.m. called my credit union. I need
to report fraud. Complete records of all
transactions for 6 months. Ashley
Porter, the representative, sympathized.
I'm so sorry, Mrs. Holloway. We'll
investigate immediately. I need
documentation of the power of attorney
documents submitted to authorize these
transfers.
You'll have those within three business
days.
10 a.m. Call Davidson, a retired
attorney from my book club. David, my
son stole my savings and fraudulently
sold my property. I need legal
representation.
Pause. How much are we talking?
127,000 in theft. 340,000 in fraudulent
sale proceeds.
Jesus. Yes, I'll help you. my office at
2 p.m. 11:00 a.m. Called Tom Henderson
at 1247 Oak Street. Tom, I need to tell
you something upsetting. My son sold
your house without my permission using
fraudulent documents.
Silence. He what? I explained calmly.
Tom's voice was tight with anger. Some
stranger thinks they bought our house.
Your lease is valid and binding. You're
protected. But I wanted you to know
immediately. I may need you to provide
statements.
Whatever you need, Mrs. Holloway.
Absolutely whatever you need.
100 p.m. Called Patricia Summers, my
best friend. Patricia, can you come
over? I need to tell you something. On
my way. At 2 p.m. I sat in David
Richardson's office on Third Street. He
listened to my story without
interrupting. When I finished, he
removed his glasses. Beatatrice, your
son has committed multiple felonies.
Grand theft, fraud, forgery. This isn't
civil. This is criminal. He could face 5
to 10 years. I know. Are you sure about
this? He's your son. I met his eyes. He
stole my life savings and tried to make
me homeless. What kind of son does that?
David pulled out a legal pad. First,
file fraud reports. You've started.
Second, contact the title company.
Third, file a police report. You can
request investigation before pressing
charges. What about the buyers? They'll
sue Connor for fraud. He'll be liable
for the full purchase price plus
damages. Good. Beatrice, what do you
want? Punishment? Restitution. I thought
about Connor<unk>s casual voice, so
certain I wouldn't fight back. I want
justice. I want every penny back. and I
want him to understand that betrayal has
consequences.
Then let's build an ironclad case. We
spent 2 hours preparing documents. By
the time I left, I'd filed fraud claims
with my credit union and the title
company. The police report would be
filed October 29th. My phone buzzed.
Connor, mom, why is the bank calling
about fraud? What's going on? Me. The
bank flagged the withdrawals as
suspicious. Standard procedure, Connor.
But I'm your son. Tell them it's fine.
Me? Is it fine, Connor? You took
without permission. Connor, I had your
permission. I borrowed it. We'll pay you
back. I stared at borrowed as if you
could borrow without asking. I didn't
respond. That evening, Patricia had wine
poured and tissues ready. I told her
everything. Her expression shifted from
shock to fury to something like awe.
That absolute bastard. She said,
"Maggie, I'm here for whatever you need.
Can you help me organize my
documentation? Bank statements, lease
agreements, property deeds. I need
everything categorized and copied.
Consider it done." We sat drinking wine
as October sun set outside. For the
first time since that terrible call, I
felt something other than betrayal. I
felt determination. You know what the
worst part is? I said softly. Not the
money, not the house. It's that he
thought I was too stupid or weak to
fight back.
Patricia squeezed my hand. He's about to
learn his mother is neither stupid nor
weak.
That night, Connor texted, "Mom, please
come to the wedding tomorrow. I want you
there. Let's not fight over money." I
stared at the message for a long time.
then typed, "I'll be there. Let him
think everything was fine. Let him marry
his calculating bride and celebrate with
my stolen money. Let him believe he'd
gotten away with it."
I would smile. I would take photos. I
would play the proud mother. And then
when he least expected it, I would show
him exactly what happened when you
betrayed Beatatrice Holloway. October
The wedding was exactly what I expected,
expensive, showy, and paid for entirely
with my stolen money. Riverside Country
Club glittered with white roses and
crystal chandeliers. I arrived at 2 p.m.
wearing my navy blue dress, elegant,
understated, and carrying a small gift
wrapped in silver paper. Inside was a
framed photo of Connor as a baby, one
Robert had taken. I'd written on the
back. May you always remember where you
came from. Love, Mom. Vanessa looked
stunning in a designer gown that
probably cost $12,000.
Connor stood beside her in a tuxedo,
beaming like he didn't have a care in
the world. When he saw me, he rushed
over. Mom, you came. He hugged me
tightly. I knew you'd understand. This
is a fresh start for all of us. I pulled
back, studying his face. Did he really
believe that? Congratulations, Connor.
You look very happy. Vanessa joined us,
her smile sharp as broken glass.
Beatatric, so glad you're not being
difficult. Connor was worried, but I
told him, "You don't need that big house
anymore anyway. We can build our future
now." "How thoughtful," I replied, my
tone pleasant, but my eyes cold. The
ceremony was brief. I sat in the front
row, watching my son promise to love and
cherish this woman who'd helped him
betray his own mother. Around me, 63
guests, I counted, laughed, and dabbed
at tears. Vanessa's parents, Frank and
Diane Pierce, sat across the aisle.
Diane kept glancing at me, her
expression strangely guilty. During the
reception, I made polite conversation
with strangers, drank champagne I didn't
taste, and took photos I'd never want to
see again. The room sparkled with 300
white roses at $40 per arrangement,
The champagne was Vuv Cleico at $200 per
bottle. 15 bottles visible, $3,000. The
four tier cake with sugar flowers,
I knew these prices because I'd helped
plan Robert's company parties for years.
This wedding cost at least $75,000.
Just as they cut the cake, my phone
buzzed. A text from David Richardson.
Police investigation opened. Fraud
charges being prepared. Title company
confirms notary seal fraudulent. Notary
was in California on September 16th, not
Ohio Hospital. You have him. I looked at
Connor laughing with his groomsmen,
completely oblivious. Not yet, I
thought. Let him enjoy this moment. It
would be his last happy one for a very
long time.
October 31st, Halloween. How fitting.
Connor and Vanessa appeared at my door
at 856 Maple Drive, unannounced. Connor
looked furious. Vanessa's expression was
icy calculation.
What the hell did you do? Connor
demanded, pushing past me into my living
room. The title company froze the money
from the house sale. They're saying the
power of attorney was forged. They're
threatening criminal charges. I closed
the door calmly. Perhaps because it was
forged. You signed those papers. I was
there. I signed blank papers while
delirious with a fever of 104°.
You took advantage of your dying mother.
Vanessa stepped forward. This is
ridiculous, Beatatric. You're old. You
can't manage your finances anymore. We
were helping you by stealing $127,000.
It's not stealing if you're too scenile
to manage it yourself. Her mask slipped,
revealing cold opportunism beneath.
Connor is your son. Everything you have
should go to him eventually anyway. We
just needed it now. I kept my voice
steady. So, this was your plan? Find a
man with a wealthy parent, manipulate
him into stealing from her, then build
your life on her money. Connor<unk>'s
face flushed. Don't talk about Vanessa
like that. She loves me. She loves your
inheritance. I corrected. Or what she
thought was your inheritance. Vanessa
laughed harsh and ugly. You think you're
so smart? Drop these accusations or
we'll make your life hell. We'll tell
everyone you have dementia. We'll have
you declared incompetent. We'll put you
in a nursing home. Is that a threat?
It's a promise, Connor said, his voice
low. Mom, I'm trying to be nice here.
Just tell the bank and title company you
made a mistake. Tell them you authorized
everything. And if I don't, Connor
stepped closer, looming over me. Then
you'll find out how difficult your life
can become. We have lawyers, too. We'll
tie you up in court for years. We'll
drain whatever money you have left.
We'll make sure everyone knows you're a
confused old woman who can't remember
what she signed.
For a moment, I felt genuine fear. Not
of their threats. I had evidence on my
side, but of what my son had become.
This angry, threatening man was a
stranger. But I had not survived 37
years alone by being weak. "Get out of
my house," I said, my voice like steel.
Both of you now. Mom, get out. You have
5 seconds. 1 2 They left. Vanessa
spitting curses. Connor<unk>'s face
twisted with rage. As their car pulled
away, I sat down heavily, hands shaking.
The mask was off. Now I knew exactly who
I was dealing with. I called David
Richardson. File the police report.
Press full charges. I want them
prosecuted to the fullest extent of the
law. Are you sure? Completely. The next
week moved quickly. Detective James
Morrison, 45, took my statement on
November 2nd. He was thorough,
professional, and increasingly disturbed
by what he heard. Mrs. Holloway, I've
seen a lot of elder financial abuse
cases. This is one of the most
calculated I've encountered. The credit
union provided their findings on
November 4th. The power of attorney's
documents showed multiple
irregularities.
The notary seal belonged to Margaret
Phillips, whose license expired August
Margaret Phillips was in Sacramento,
California on September 16th. Her credit
card records proved it. My signature
appeared shaky, inconsistent with my
normal handwriting. The documents were
dated September 16th, but weren't filed
with the bank until September 22nd.
William Bradford, CEO of Pinnacle Title
Company, called on November 5th. Mrs.
Holloway, I owe you an apology. We
failed to catch these irregularities.
We've frozen all proceeds from the sale,
Mr. Holloway won't see a penny. What
about the buyers? The Martinez family?
They're devastated. They're suing your
son for fraud, breach of contract, and
emotional distress. They put their
entire life savings into that down
payment. Guilt twisted in my stomach.
Can you give me their contact
information? I'd like to speak with
them. I'll have my assistant send it
over. That afternoon, I called Rosa
Martinez. She answered, her voice
cautious. Mrs. Holloway. Mrs. Martinez,
I'm so deeply sorry. I had no idea my
son would do this. Your family is
innocent in all of this. Rosa's voice
cracked. We have three children, Mrs.
Holloway. We saved for 12 years to buy
that house. We thought we thought we
finally had our dream home. I promise
you, your lease will be honored. The
Hendersons will move out when their
lease ends in June 2026.
After that, if you still want the house,
I'll sell it to you at the price you
silence, then soft crying. Thank you.
Thank you so much.
Patricia came over that evening with
wine and support. We organized my
documentation into three complete sets.
One for David, one for the police, one
for me. Maggie, how are you holding up?
She asked. I'm angry. I'm hurt. But I'm
also clear about what needs to happen.
No doubts? None. Connor made his choice.
Now he lives with the consequences.
November 8th, 2024. I was at the library
shelving returns in the biography
section when my phone chimed. An email
from an unknown address. Truthteller
20224.protonmail.com.
Subject line, you should know the truth
about Connor. My hands trembled as I
opened it. Mrs. Holloway, I'm writing
anonymously because I'm afraid of
retaliation, but you deserve to know.
Connor has a 5-year-old daughter named
Emma Rose Holloway. The mother is Sarah
Mitchell. They dated in college. Sarah
got pregnant their senior year. Connor
told her to get an abortion. When she
refused, he abandoned her. Sarah sued
for child support 2 years ago. The court
ordered Connor to pay 1,500 per month
plus $80,000 in back support. Vanessa
doesn't know about Emma. Connor has been
secretly taking money from you to pay
Sarah off and make her go away. Emma
deserves better. So do you, a concerned
friend. I stood frozen in the biography
aisle, surrounded by the life stories of
strangers while my son's secret exploded
in my hands. A granddaughter. I had a
granddaughter. I searched Facebook on my
phone with shaking fingers. Sarah
Mitchell, Columbus, Ohio. Her profile
was partially public. The most recent
photo posted 3 days ago. A little girl
with dark curls and Connor<unk>'s exact
smile wearing a purple dress blowing out
five candles on a birthday cake. The
caption read, "Happy fth birthday to my
Emma Rose. You're my whole world, even
if your father refuses to acknowledge
you exist." I felt my knees weaken.
Patricia found me 20 minutes later
sitting on the floor between the
shelves, phone in my hand, tears
streaming down my face. Maggie, what
happened? I showed her the email. The
photos. Oh my god, Patricia whispered.
You have a granddaughter. November 10th,
I drove to Columbus with Patricia. Sarah
Mitchell lived in a small apartment
complex on Parson's Avenue, unit 2C. I
knocked at 2:00 p.m. as we'd arranged by
phone. Sarah opened the door. 33 years
old, tired eyes, but kind face. Mrs.
Holloway, please come in. The apartment
was tiny, but spotlessly clean.
Children's drawings covered the
refrigerator. A small bookshelf held
worn copies of Dr. Seuss and chapter
books. "Thank you for agreeing to see
me," I said. "I never wanted to destroy
Connor," Sarah said quietly. I just
wanted help raising Emma. She asks about
her father every single day. Where is
she now? Kindergarten. She'll be home at
3:30. Sarah poured coffee with shaking
hands. Mrs. Holloway, I need you to
know. I loved Connor once. He was
charming, ambitious, kind. Or I thought
he was. When I got pregnant, he changed
overnight. Told me I was ruining his
life. offered me $10,000 to take care of
it. I'm so sorry. When I kept Emma, he
disappeared, blocked my number, changed
jobs so I couldn't find him. I struggled
alone for 3 years before I finally hired
a lawyer and sued for support. The
Sarah nodded. "3 years of back support
plus legal fees. The court ordered it.
Connor paid 50,000 last year. I think
that's when he started stealing from
you. Then suddenly, 2 months ago, he
paid the remaining 30,000.
I thought maybe he'd finally grown up.
She laughed bitterly. I had no idea he
was using his mother's money.
At 3:45, we heard small footsteps on the
stairs. The door burst open. Mommy. Emma
Rose Holloway was beautiful. Dark curly
hair like Sarah's, but Connor<unk>'s
brown eyes, Connor<unk>'s smile,
Robert's nose. She wore a pink backpack
covered in unicorn stickers. She stopped
when she saw strangers in her living
room. Who are they? Sarah knelt down.
Emma, this is Mrs. Holloway. She's your
She's your grandmother. Your daddy's
mother. Emma's eyes went wide. My
daddy's mommy. I couldn't speak. I just
nodded. Emma walked over cautiously.
Mommy says my daddy lives far away. Do
you know where he is? Yes, sweetheart. I
know where he is. Does he Does he think
about me sometimes?
My heart shattered. I think he thinks
about you all the time. He's just He's
made some mistakes.
Grown-up mistakes.
Emma studied my face with those serious
5-year-old eyes. Mommy says everyone
makes mistakes. She says the important
thing is saying sorry and trying to be
better. Your mother is very wise. Emma
suddenly smiled. Connor<unk>'s smile and
my heart broke all over again. Do you
want to see my drawings? I'm really good
at cats.
November 15th, 2024. Preliminary hearing
at Franklin County Courthouse. Connor
appeared with his attorney, James
Sullivan, a sharp dressed defense lawyer
specializing in white collar crime. I
arrived with David Richardson. Detective
Morrison was there. Sarah Mitchell sat
in the back row, Emma at her
grandmother's house for the day. Connor
looked terrible. Dark circles under his
eyes, suit wrinkled, hands shaking. When
his eyes met mine, I saw genuine fear.
Judge Helen Pritchard, 62, presided. She
reviewed the evidence David presented.
Bank records, fraudulent power of
attorney documents, title company
findings, detectives report. Mr.
Holloway. Judge Pritchard said the
evidence here is overwhelming. The
prosecution is prepared to file formal
charges of grand theft, fraud, and
forgery. Do you understand the
seriousness of these allegations?
Yes, your honor, Connor whispered.
Before we proceed, does anyone wish to
make a statement? I stood. Your honor,
may I speak? Proceed, Mrs. Holloway. I
looked directly at Connor. Connor,
before this goes further, tell me about
Emma. Connor<unk>s face went white as
snow. Vanessa, sitting behind him,
looked confused. Emma? Who's Emma?
Connor? I said quietly. Tell them about
your daughter. The courtroom went
silent. Connor<unk>s attorney leaned
over, whispering urgently. Connor shook
his head, tears suddenly streaming down
his face. "Your honor," Connor said, his
voice breaking. I I need to make a
statement. Judge Pritchard looked
surprised. Go ahead, Mr. Holloway.
Connor stood shaking. I have a
5-year-old daughter named Emma Rose. Her
mother is Sarah Mitchell. I I abandoned
them when Sarah was pregnant. I
pretended she didn't exist because I was
ashamed. Because Vanessa's family is
wealthy and wouldn't accept a man with a
secret child. Vanessa gasped behind him.
What? Two years ago, Sarah sued me for
child support. The court ordered me to
pay $80,000 in back support. I didn't
have it. I panicked. He turned to me,
tears flowing freely. Mom, I needed that
money to pay Sarah off, to make her go
away, to hide my mistakes. So I So I
took yours. The truth hung in the air
like smoke. I stole from my mother to
cover up the fact that I abandoned my
daughter. I'm a coward. I'm a thief. I
don't deserve mercy. He looked at
Vanessa, who was staring at him with
horror and disgust. I don't deserve
love. I don't deserve family.
He collapsed back into his chair,
sobbing. Vanessa stood abruptly.
I can't
I can't do this. She walked out of the
courtroom, her heels clicking on marble.
Judge Pritchard studied Connor for a
long moment. Mr. Holloway, I appreciate
your honesty, however belated. We'll
recess for 30 minutes. I suggest you use
that time to speak with your attorney
about your options. As the courtroom
emptied, I walked over to Connor. He
looked up at me, face wet with tears.
"Mom, I'm so sorry. I'm so so sorry,
Connor," I said quietly. Sorry isn't
enough. Sorry is just words. You need to
become the man your daughter deserves.
The man your father raised you to be.
How? How do I fix this? You start by
facing the consequences of your choices.
All of them. I walked away, leaving my
son broken in that courtroom. Patricia
met me in the hallway. Maggie, are you
okay? No, I said, but I will be. because
now I knew the full truth. And the
truth, however painful, was the only way
forward.
November 20th, 2024, 5 days before
Thanksgiving. I was in my kitchen at 856
Maple Drive, making tea, when the
doorbell rang. A woman stood on my
porch, late 50s, elegant silver hair,
expensive coat. She looked familiar, but
I couldn't place her. Mrs. Holloway. I'm
Diane Pierce, Vanessa's mother. May I
May I please speak with you? I
considered closing the door. Instead, I
stepped back. Come in. We sat in my
living room. Diane's hands shook as she
pulled an envelope from her purse. I
need to give you this. I should have
given it to you years ago. What is it?
The truth. She handed me the envelope.
Please read it. Then I'll explain
everything. I opened the envelope.
Inside was a letter handwritten on cream
colored stationery dated November 18th,
Dear Mrs. Holloway, 23 years ago on
February 14th, 2001, your husband Robert
saved my life. I was driving on Route 71
with my six-year-old daughter Vanessa in
the back seat when I lost control of my
car during an ice storm. We spun into
oncoming traffic. Robert was driving
toward us. He could have swerved away,
saved himself. Instead, he deliberately
steered his car to take the impact,
protecting us. His car absorbed the
collision. Vanessa and I walked away
with minor injuries. Robert James
Holloway died so that my daughter could
